name: Money Sense — Profit, Loss, Interest and Budgets
whatIsIt: Everyday money maths. PROFIT = selling price − cost price (when you sell for more than you paid); LOSS is the opposite. A BUDGET plans money: income − expenses = savings. SIMPLE INTEREST is what a bank pays (or a loan costs): Interest = Principal × Rate × Time ÷ 100. These turn percentages and subtraction into real financial decisions.
whyNeeded: Managing money is a life skill everyone needs: running a small stall, saving pocket money, understanding a bank account, or not overspending. It's also the foundation of commerce, economics and business studies.
realLifeUses: A shopkeeper working out profit on items sold
Planning pocket money: spend some, save some
Interest earned on money kept in a bank
Deciding if a 'sale' price is really a good deal

vocabulary.meaning: What you paid (CP) and what you sell for (SP).
Profit = SP − CP (gain); Loss = CP − SP (when SP is lower).

story.title: Anaya's Lemonade Stall
story.text: Anaya spends ₹120 on lemons and sugar (her cost price) and sells all the lemonade for ₹200. 'Did you make money?' asks Papa. She works out: 200 − 120 = ₹80. 'I made ₹80 profit!' Papa adds, 'and if you save it in the bank at interest, it can even grow.' Anaya decides to save half and reinvest half.
story.extractedProblem: Anaya's cost price is ₹120 and she sells for ₹200. What is her profit?
story.answerInStory: ₹80 — because profit = selling price − cost price = 200 − 120.

visual.caption: Selling price splits into cost price plus profit: 120 + 80 = 200. Profit = SP − CP.

teachingGallery.title: Profit, budget, interest
teachingGallery.note: Profit = SP − CP; savings = income − expenses; SI = P×R×T/100.

standardMethod.summary: Compare selling and cost prices for profit/loss; plan a budget as income − expenses; use Interest = P×R×T÷100 for simple interest.
steps: Profit or loss: compare SP and CP. Profit = SP − CP; Loss = CP − SP.
Budget: savings = income − total expenses (spend less than you earn).
Simple interest: Interest = Principal × Rate × Time ÷ 100.

workedExamples.problem: A trader buys a bag for ₹250 and sells it for ₹300. Profit or loss, and how much?
steps: SP ₹300 > CP ₹250, so it's a profit.

workedExamples.problem: Find the simple interest on ₹2000 at 5% per year for 2 years.

commonMistakes.mistake: Calling it a profit when the selling price is LOWER than the cost price.
commonMistakes.fix: Profit only when SP > CP. If SP < CP it's a LOSS. Compare the two prices first.

commonMistakes.mistake: Forgetting to divide by 100 in the simple interest formula.
commonMistakes.fix: Rate is a percentage, so divide by 100: Interest = P × R × T ÷ 100.

easy.q: Cost price ₹80, selling price ₹100. What is the profit (in ₹)?
If selling price is LESS than cost price, it is a…
hintLadder: You got back less than you paid.

medium.q: Income ₹600, expenses ₹450. How much is saved (in ₹)?
Simple interest on ₹1000 at 10% for 1 year (in ₹)?

challenge.q: A cycle costs ₹2000 and is sold for ₹1700. What is the loss (in ₹)?
Simple interest on ₹5000 at 4% for 2 years (in ₹)?

questions.q: Simple interest on ₹2000 at 5% for 1 year (in ₹)?

teachBackPrompt: Plan a tiny budget with a grown-up: imagine ₹200 pocket money, decide what to spend and what to save, and explain income − expenses = savings. Then work out the profit if you bought a snack for ₹10 and 'sold' it to a sibling for ₹15.

revisionCard.summary: Profit = SP − CP (only when SP > CP); Loss = CP − SP. Budget: savings = income − expenses. Simple interest = Principal × Rate × Time ÷ 100 (don't forget ÷100). Amount back = principal + interest.

meta.curriculum: CBSE/NCERT Class 7 (money maths — profit/loss, simple interest, budgeting)
